source_paper_title: New Combinatorial Insights for Monotone Apportionment
context:
Section 6 (Discussion), page 23

Whether a similar result holds without violating the house size is open.
title: Randomized apportionment with fixed house size satisfying population monotonicity, quota, and ex-ante proportionality
background_latex:
- An apportionment instance consists of a population vector \(p=(p_1,\dots,p_n)\in \mathbb{N}^n\) and a house size \(H\in\mathbb{N}\). Let \(P=\sum_{i=1}^n p_i\) be the total population.
- An apportionment outcome is a (possibly randomized) vector \(X=(X_1,\dots,X_n)\in \mathbb{N}_0^n\).
- The quota of state \(i\) is \(q_i=\frac{p_i}{P}H\).
- Fixed house size means \(\sum_{i=1}^n X_i = H\) holds almost surely.
- Quota compliance means \(X_i\in\{\lfloor q_i\rfloor,\lceil q_i\rceil\}\) almost surely for every \(i\).
- Ex-ante proportionality means \(\mathbb{E}[X_i]=q_i\) for every \(i\).
- Population monotonicity (as defined in the paper) means that for any two instances \((p,H)\) and \((p',H')\) and any pair of states \(i,j\), if \(p'_i/p'_j\ge p_i/p_j\), then it does not happen that state \(i\) strictly decreases and state \(j\) strictly increases in the realized apportionment (formally, for realized outputs \(X\) at \((p,H)\) and \(X'\) at \((p',H')\), one must have either \(X_i\le X'_i\) or \(X_j\ge X'_j\), or (if ratios are equal) that \(X'\) is also feasible for \((p,H)\)).
problem_definition_latex: Determine whether there exists a randomized apportionment method that, for every instance \((p,H)\), outputs \(X\in\mathbb{N}_0^n\) with \(\sum_{i=1}^n X_i=H\) almost surely, is quota-compliant (\(X_i\in\{\lfloor q_i\rfloor,\lceil q_i\rceil\}\) almost surely for all \(i\)), is ex-ante proportional (\(\mathbb{E}[X_i]=q_i\) for all \(i\)), and is population-monotone (in the sense defined above).
latex:
- An apportionment instance consists of a population vector \(p=(p_1,\dots,p_n)\in \mathbb{N}^n\) and a house size \(H\in\mathbb{N}\). Let \(P=\sum_{i=1}^n p_i\) be the total population.
- An apportionment outcome is a (possibly randomized) vector \(X=(X_1,\dots,X_n)\in \mathbb{N}_0^n\).
- The quota of state \(i\) is \(q_i=\frac{p_i}{P}H\).
- Fixed house size means \(\sum_{i=1}^n X_i = H\) holds almost surely.
- Quota compliance means \(X_i\in\{\lfloor q_i\rfloor,\lceil q_i\rceil\}\) almost surely for every \(i\).
- Ex-ante proportionality means \(\mathbb{E}[X_i]=q_i\) for every \(i\).
- Population monotonicity (as defined in the paper) means that for any two instances \((p,H)\) and \((p',H')\) and any pair of states \(i,j\), if \(p'_i/p'_j\ge p_i/p_j\), then it does not happen that state \(i\) strictly decreases and state \(j\) strictly increases in the realized apportionment (formally, for realized outputs \(X\) at \((p,H)\) and \(X'\) at \((p',H')\), one must have either \(X_i\le X'_i\) or \(X_j\ge X'_j\), or (if ratios are equal) that \(X'\) is also feasible for \((p,H)\)).

Determine whether there exists a randomized apportionment method that, for every instance \((p,H)\), outputs \(X\in\mathbb{N}_0^n\) with \(\sum_{i=1}^n X_i=H\) almost surely, is quota-compliant (\(X_i\in\{\lfloor q_i\rfloor,\lceil q_i\rceil\}\) almost surely for all \(i\)), is ex-ante proportional (\(\mathbb{E}[X_i]=q_i\) for all \(i\)), and is population-monotone (in the sense defined above).
open_problem_quote: Whether a similar result holds without violating the house size is open.
